cards.hook: He built one of electronica's most revered albums in his Cornwall bedroom, some tracks made as a teenager
cards.story: Richard D. James grew up in Lanner, Cornwall, far from any music-industry hub. In 1991 he co-founded Rephlex Records with Grant Wilson-Claridge, and in 1992 released Selected Ambient Works 85–92 — home-recorded ambient techno that became a landmark of the genre. The distance from the industry may be exactly what let his sound stay so singular.

media.label: Selected Ambient Works Volume II — Full Album

cards.hook: After 13 years of near-silence under the Aphex name, one album broke cover and won a Grammy
cards.story: Following Drukqs in 2001, Aphex Twin largely went quiet, releasing under other aliases instead. Then Syro arrived in 2014 as his first Aphex Twin album in 13 years. It went on to win the Grammy for Best Dance/Electronic Album.

sources.title: Grammy Award for Best Dance/Electronic Album

cards.hook: The grinning-face era: a video so unsettling it became a landmark of the form
cards.story: In 1999 Aphex Twin released Windowlicker on Warp, its video directed by Chris Cunningham. The pairing followed their earlier work on Come to Daddy in 1997. Both clips fused James's music with Cunningham's disturbing, digitally-morphed imagery and are widely cited as landmarks of music video.

cards.hook: His pattern held: after a long silence, a sudden 2023 return that earned another Grammy nod
cards.story: Aphex Twin has long alternated long silences with bursts of generosity. In 2023 he released Blackbox Life Recorder 21f. The track went on to earn a Grammy nomination, extending a career that has stayed unpredictable while remaining beloved.

sources.title: Blackbox Life Recorder 21f / In a Room7 F760
Grammy Award for Best Dance/Electronic Recording

cards.hook: A one-man universe from Las Vegas: how Aplete became Stefano Kajatt's whole creative world
cards.story: Aplete is the solo project of Stefano Kajatt, a Las Vegas multi-instrumentalist who also leads the band and recording collective Trust in the Sun. The name is bigger than a track list: it's where Kajatt shares songs first, but also art, ideas and works-in-progress under One Sky Ally, his umbrella for creative projects. The streaming catalogue is small and deliberate, while the deeper well of sketches and covers lives on SoundCloud.

cards.hook: The first Aplete song you can stream is a 2022 debut called Little Bird
cards.story: Aplete's streaming story starts in 2022 with 'Little Bird,' the earliest of just three deliberate releases on the official catalogue. It was followed by 'You Better Lay Low' with Kella T. in 2023 and 'FIOH, Vol. 1' in 2024. The trio keeps the discography intentionally small, with everything more exploratory living on SoundCloud.

media.label: You Better Lay Low with Kella T. (official video)

cards.hook: Aplete keeps turning up in other people's records, from River Rhythm to Christina Lakis
cards.story: Beyond his own catalogue, Kajatt shows up across a web of collaborations. He appears with River Rhythm on 'Lotus Pond' and 'Mystery of Life,' with Red Grooves on 'When We All Are One,' with Souza on 'Closure,' and with Christina Lakis on 'Tunnel Vision.'

cards.hook: Atoms for Peace exists because Thom Yorke wanted to play his solo album live
cards.story: Thom Yorke's 2006 solo album The Eraser was built almost entirely alone, and it needed a band to exist on stage. In 2009 he assembled one: producer Nigel Godrich, Red Hot Chili Peppers bassist Flea, drummer Joey Waronker and percussionist Mauro Refosco. They played their first shows that October and toured the US through 2010.

cards.hook: The band's name traces back to an Eisenhower speech — by way of a song title
cards.story: Atoms for Peace took its name from a track on The Eraser, which itself references Dwight D. Eisenhower's 1953 'Atoms for Peace' speech to the United Nations. The one album they made together, Amok (2013), reached No. 5 on the UK charts.

cards.hook: The song she wrote at eleven became a slow-burning global phenomenon
cards.story: Before she was AURORA the pop export, she was a kid in western Norway writing songs almost in secret. One of them, "Runaway," she wrote at age eleven — and years later it quietly grew into one of her most-loved tracks, finding new life with a devoted fanbase she calls her warriors and weirdos. It anchored her 2016 debut, All My Demons Greeting Me as a Friend.

sources.title: All My Demons Greeting Me as a Friend
AURORA opens up about 'Runaway' – the song that inspired Billie Eilish

cards.hook: Her Oasis cover was the sound of a John Lewis Christmas
cards.story: In 2015, AURORA's fragile, glacial take on Oasis's "Half the World Away" soundtracked the John Lewis Christmas advert — a moment that pushed her voice into millions of British living rooms. The song was originally written by Noel Gallagher and released by Oasis in the 1990s. For AURORA it became an early, unexpected breakthrough.

media.label: AURORA — Half the World Away (John Lewis 2015)

sources.title: All My Demons Greeting Me as a Friend

cards.hook: That wordless call luring Elsa in Frozen II? That is AURORA
cards.story: The haunting siren-like voice that beckons Elsa in Disney's Frozen II belongs to AURORA — the song "Into the Unknown" was performed by Idina Menzel featuring AURORA. She has also lent her voice to animation beyond Disney, recording a reworked version of her song "Running with the Wolves" for the acclaimed film WolfWalkers. Both roles put her unmistakable tone at the emotional core of the films.

media.label: Search: AURORA Into the Unknown Frozen 2

sources.title: Aurora On 'Frozen 2's 'Into the Unknown': Emerging Artists Spotlight
AURORA Voices The North Wind in Frozen 2

cards.hook: She rebuilt the Greek pantheon as glittering art-pop
cards.story: On The Gods We Can Touch (2022), AURORA drew on Greek mythology to frame an album of shimmering, theatrical art-pop. The record marked a bolder, more playful turn after her earlier electro-folk.

cards.hook: T-Bone Burnett signed Autolux to a label he ran with the Coen Brothers
cards.story: Autolux formed in Los Angeles in 2001, and their self-released debut EP caught the ear of producer T-Bone Burnett, who signed the trio to DMZ — a small label he created with the Coen Brothers — and personally nurtured the band through their first album, Future Perfect.

cards.hook: Autolux's drummer has spent years behind Jack White's kit too
cards.story: Carla Azar, Autolux's drummer and one of its lead vocalists, has drummed on three Jack White solo albums — Blunderbuss, Lazaretto and Boarding House Reach — and toured live with him. It's the kind of double life that has let Autolux release only three albums across two-plus decades without the band ever really stopping.

cards.hook: Three jazz students covered Odd Future — and the rappers came calling
cards.story: Chester Hansen, Matthew Tavares and Alexander Sowinski met in 2010 in Humber College's jazz program in Toronto and started posting jazz reworkings of hip-hop tracks, including Odd Future material. The covers went viral, Tyler, the Creator amplified them, and within a few years the trio had gone from covering rap records to making one: Sour Soul, a full album with Ghostface Killah, in 2015.

media.label: BBNG x Tyler, the Creator session

sources.title: Ghostface Killah & BADBADNOTGOOD: Sour Soul review

cards.hook: For Talk Memory, they flew in the Brazilian legend who scored their record collection
cards.story: BADBADNOTGOOD's Talk Memory (2021) put the band side by side with Arthur Verocai — the cult Brazilian composer whose 1972 debut they had long cited as an influence. Verocai contributed string arrangements across the record and appears on 'Beside April', the kind of full-circle collaboration crate-diggers dream about.

cards.hook: A belly-dance scoring project quietly grew into the Bay Area's mechanical circus
cards.story: Beats Antique began in 2007 as a studio collaboration between producers David Satori and Tommy "Sidecar" Cappel and dancer-percussionist Zoe Jakes, originally making music for belly dance. The format couldn't contain them for long — the trio soon fused Middle Eastern and Balkan traditions with hip-hop drums and heavy electronics, and turned the live show into a theatrical spectacle with Jakes out front.

sources.title: Beats Antique: Organic and Electronic, Heavy Bass and Belly Dancing
Beats Antique on Apple Music (editorial bio)

cards.hook: Les Claypool crawls through a Beats Antique track called Beelzebub
cards.story: Beats Antique's 2010 album Blind Threshold pushed their theatrical streak further, and one of its most talked-about moments is the single Beelzebub, featuring Les Claypool. His slithering low-end fit the band's dark, circus-like world perfectly.

cards.hook: Their debut sketched a genre by colliding Balkan brass with hip-hop drums
cards.story: Beats Antique's 2007 debut Tribal Derivations set the template for everything that followed: Middle Eastern and Balkan brass traditions welded to hip-hop beats and modern electronic production. It's the record that hinted at a genre the band has been happily distorting ever since.

cards.hook: Critics couldn't file them under any genre, so the label stuck came from one of the band's own song titles
cards.story: Formed in Nashville in the late 1980s, Béla Fleck and the Flecktones fused bluegrass banjo with jazz harmony, funk grooves, and world rhythms into a sound that fit no existing box. The band even had a tune called Blu-Bop, and the coinage became shorthand for what they do — proof that the uncategorizable could still find an audience.

media.label: Sex In A Pan — Live at The Cap
Search: Flight of the Cosmic Hippo

sources.title: Béla Fleck and the Flecktones — MusicBrainz record

cards.hook: The Flecktones' percussionist doesn't play drums — he plays a guitar-shaped electronic instrument he built himself
cards.story: Roy Wooten, who performs as Future Man, is the brother of bassist Victor Wooten. Instead of a traditional kit, he plays the Drumitar, a guitar-shaped electronic percussion controller of his own design that lets him trigger drum sounds with his fingers. It became one of the most distinctive visual and sonic signatures of the band.

cards.hook: A banjo-driven Christmas record won this band a Grammy
cards.story: In 2008 the Flecktones released Jingle All the Way, a holiday album that ran carols through their signature blend of bluegrass, jazz, and funk. It went on to win the Grammy for Best Pop Instrumental Album, an unlikely trophy for a Christmas record led by a banjo. It stands among the band's multiple Grammy honors.

media.label: Search: Jingle All the Way Flecktones

sources.title: Jingle All the Way (Béla Fleck and the Flecktones album)
Grammy Award for Best Pop Instrumental Album

cards.hook: It took roughly two decades to get the original four Flecktones back in one room — and the result was Rocket Science
cards.story: Harmonica and keyboard player Howard Levy left the Flecktones in 1992, and for years the band carried on with other members, including saxophonist Jeff Coffin. Levy's return brought the original quartet back together for 2011's Rocket Science, a reunion nearly twenty years in the making.

sources.title: Rocket Science (Béla Fleck and the Flecktones album)

cards.hook: The album of volcanic strings that many still call Björk's masterpiece
cards.story: After Debut and Post opened the doors between pop and electronica, Homogenic (1997) fused abrasive beats with sweeping strings into something singular. It's the album that fixed her reputation as a genuine innovator.

cards.hook: Her most jubilant, big-band hit isn't even her own song
cards.story: It's Oh So Quiet, the brass-blasting showstopper from Post (1995), is a cover — a reworking of an older song rather than a Björk original. Its unforgettable video, all swirling dance and sudden bursts of color, was directed by Spike Jonze. The contrast between whispered verses and explosive choruses made it one of her most beloved singles.

media.label: Björk — It's Oh So Quiet (official video)

sources.title: One Great Cover: Bjork's "It's Oh So Quiet"

cards.hook: She was a recording artist before she was a teenager
cards.story: Björk released her first solo album while still a child, long before the world knew her name. She spent her youth passing through Icelandic bands before fronting the alt-pop iconoclasts The Sugarcubes. Only in 1993, with Debut, did she step out fully on her own — arriving just as the walls between pop, electronic and avant-garde music were coming down.

cards.hook: Two robots fall in love in the video that ends Homogenic
cards.story: The closing track of Homogenic, All Is Full of Love, became famous less for its sound than for its vision: a video directed by Chris Cunningham in which two white robots, built in Björk's likeness, tenderly assemble and embrace. It remains one of the most acclaimed music videos ever made, a landmark meeting of pop and industrial design.

media.label: Björk — All Is Full of Love (official video)

sources.title: How Bjork's robots influenced the way we think about the future

cards.hook: Shot days before a peace concert, he played anyway — then left Jamaica and made his masterpiece
cards.story: In December 1976 gunmen stormed Bob Marley's Kingston home and wounded him just before the Smile Jamaica concert. He took the stage two days later despite his injuries, then went into self-imposed exile in London. There, in a country far from home, he recorded Exodus — the album that turned him into a global figure.

cards.hook: Time magazine called this record the best album of the entire 20th century
cards.story: Exodus, released in 1977, gathered anthems like 'Jamming', 'One Love/People Get Ready' and the title track into one record made during Marley's London exile. In 1999 Time magazine named it the best album of the 20th century. It remains the door many listeners walk through to reach reggae.

media.label: Search: Bob Marley Exodus full album

sources.title: Bob Marley & The Wailers' 'Exodus' | For The Record

cards.hook: His most famous history lesson was released two years after he died
cards.story: 'Buffalo Soldier' draws a line between Black identity and the African-American cavalry regiments who fought in the 19th-century American West. Co-written by Marley with Noel Williams, known as King Sporty, it appeared posthumously on Confrontation in 1983. It became one of his most recognizable songs even though he never released it in his lifetime.

sources.title: Confrontation (Bob Marley and the Wailers album)

cards.hook: In 2024 a Hollywood biopic brought his story back to the big screen
cards.story: Bob Marley: One Love, released in 2024, dramatizes the era around the Smile Jamaica concert and the making of Exodus. Kingsley Ben-Adir played Marley, and the film was directed by Reinaldo Marcus Green. It introduced a new generation to the man behind the songs.

media.label: Search: Bob Marley One Love trailer

cards.hook: For Emma, Forever Ago started as 500 self-released CDs from a hunting cabin
cards.story: In the winter of 2006–07, after his band broke up and his health and a relationship gave out, Justin Vernon holed up in his father's hunting cabin in northwestern Wisconsin and recorded For Emma, Forever Ago. He self-released it in July 2007; Jagjaguwar gave it a wide release in early 2008, and the cabin record became one of the defining folk albums of its generation.

cards.hook: Bon Iver won Best New Artist at the Grammys — five years after their debut
cards.story: At the 2012 Grammys, Bon Iver took home Best New Artist and Best Alternative Music Album for Bon Iver, Bon Iver — a 'new artist' whose debut had come out five years earlier. The recognition kept coming: per the Recording Academy, the band has 2 wins and 11 nominations, the most recent pair arriving in 2026 for SABLE, fABLE and 'Everything Is Peaceful Love'.

sources.title: Bon Iver — Grammy Awards artist page
Bon Iver 'Everything Is Peaceful Love' video

cards.hook: Bon Iver's first Billboard top ten came from a Taylor Swift duet
cards.story: For all the acclaim, Bon Iver's first top-ten hit on the Billboard Hot 100 arrived in 2020 through 'exile', the duet with Taylor Swift on her album folklore. The band's collaborative web keeps widening — its current lineup includes Jenn Wasner of Wye Oak, and Justin Vernon's other lives run through Big Red Machine and Volcano Choir.

media.label: Taylor Swift — exile feat. Bon Iver

cards.hook: He put out his debut in 2000 and has stayed on the same label ever since
cards.story: Bonobo is a producer who came up in the late-nineties downtempo scene. His 2000 debut 'Animal Magic' led to a deal with London's Ninja Tune, a partnership that has lasted his entire career — an unusually steady run in electronic music.

cards.hook: The 2010 album that turned a bedroom producer into a full live band with strings
cards.story: 'Black Sands' arrived in 2010 and became Bonobo's turning point, fusing jazz drumming, string arrangements and club low-end into something cinematic. It gave Bonobo the material to tour with a formidable live band rather than a laptop.

cards.hook: A Bonobo single's video stars a Bond actress dancing through an empty landscape
cards.story: 'Kerala', the lead single from 2017's 'Migration', got a striking video directed by Bison that features British actress Gemma Arterton moving through a series of glitching, repeating moments. The track and its clip helped push Bonobo further into festival-headline territory.

cards.hook: His first album since 2022 is out this September, finished at Neil Young's ranch studio
cards.story: In 2026 Bonobo announced 'Distance In Static', his first record since 'Fragments', set for release on 11 September on Ninja Tune. The album brings in collaborators including Arooj Aftab, Joy Crookes, Nilüfer Yanya and Ichiko Aoba, with much of it completed at Neil Young's Broken Arrow ranch.

sources.title: Bonobo's New Album 'Distance in Static' Release Date Announced
Bonobo reveals new album Distance In Static

cards.hook: BRAIDS self-produced their debut — and it landed on the Polaris shortlist
cards.story: BRAIDS began as four friends in Calgary before relocating to Montreal, where they self-produced their 2011 debut Native Speaker. Built from patient loops, live drums and Raphaelle Standell-Preston's soaring vocals, the album was shortlisted for the 2011 Polaris Music Prize.

cards.hook: The voice of BRAIDS fronts a second band, too
cards.story: Raphaelle Standell-Preston has never worked in just one place. Alongside BRAIDS she is one half of the Montreal electronic duo Blue Hawaii, with Alex 'Agor' Kirby — a parallel project that feeds the restless, shape-shifting quality of everything she sings.

cards.hook: For years nobody knew who Burial was — the tabloids had to go hunting
cards.story: Burial arrived faceless: his first record, the South London Boroughs EP, surfaced on Kode9's Hyperdub label in 2005, followed by a self-titled debut album in 2006 that The Wire named album of the year. William Bevan recorded anonymously until 2008, when — with the press chasing his name — Hyperdub confirmed his identity. He has kept the same low profile since, letting the music appear through the label rather than the man.

cards.hook: Untrue lost the Mercury Prize — then became one of the era's landmark records anyway
cards.story: Released in November 2007, Untrue was nominated for the 2008 Mercury Prize but didn't win. Its reputation only grew, and it is now routinely counted among the defining electronic albums of its time.

cards.hook: The recluse who won't play live keeps making tracks with Four Tet and Thom Yorke
cards.story: For an artist who avoids the spotlight, Burial keeps unusual company. He has collaborated repeatedly with Four Tet, and in 2020 he worked with Radiohead's Thom Yorke.

media.label: Search: Burial, Four Tet & Thom Yorke — Her Revolution

cards.hook: In 2024 the man who never plays live scored two feature films
cards.story: Burial's slow drip of releases lately reached the cinema. In 2024 his music featured in Andrea Arnold's film Bird, and he provided the score for Harmony Korine's Baby Invasion. Both extended his ghostly, rain-soaked sound into new visual worlds — still with no tour, no fanfare, just the work.

cards.hook: Radiohead built their own studio partly because of CAN
cards.story: CAN's influence runs unusually deep: Radiohead have named the band as a direct influence on Kid A and Amnesiac, and — inspired by CAN's own studio — built a recording space of their own. Stereolab's whole rhythmic identity leans on the motorik pulse CAN's drummer Jaki Liebezeit helped popularize.

cards.hook: CAN built its own studio and edited hours of jamming into songs
cards.story: CAN recorded obsessively in a studio the band built themselves, turning long stretches of improvisation into finished tracks through editing — a working method that made records like Tago Mago and Ege Bamyası feel composed and improvised at once. All four of the band's core instrumentalists have since died, but the label they founded, Spoon Records, keeps the catalog in print.

cards.hook: He was Manitoba until a punk singer named Manitoba threatened to sue him
cards.story: Daniel Snaith first released psychedelic, sample-heavy electronic records under the name Manitoba. In 2004, Richard 'Handsome Dick' Manitoba of the Dictators threatened legal action over the name, and Snaith rebranded as Caribou rather than fight it in court. The new name has carried every stylistic swerve since.

cards.hook: The man behind Caribou is also Dr. Snaith, with a PhD in pure mathematics
cards.story: Alongside making records, Daniel Snaith earned a doctorate in mathematics from Imperial College London, completed in 2005. His dissertation dealt with overconvergent Siegel modular symbols — a corner of number theory far from the dance floor. The precision shows up in music built like proofs but sung like confessions.

cards.hook: His sixties-tinged psych-pop record beat every Canadian album of its year
cards.story: Andorra, released in 2007, was a sunburst of psychedelic pop that stood apart from the club direction he'd later take. It won the 2008 Polaris Music Prize, the award given to the best Canadian album judged on artistic merit alone. It remains one of Caribou's most acclaimed statements.

cards.hook: The closest thing modern electronic music has to a group hug came from Our Love
cards.story: Can't Do Without You appeared on Caribou's 2014 album Our Love, built from a looping, swelling vocal phrase that keeps opening wider. Its warmth turned it into one of his best-loved tracks and a live centerpiece. It captured the album's whole theme: love, connection, and generosity.

cards.hook: The first streaming-only album ever to win a Grammy — and he stayed unsigned to do it
cards.story: Coloring Book arrived free in May 2016, a choir-and-horns celebration of faith recorded after Chance's turn on Kanye West's The Life of Pablo. It became the first streaming-only release to win a Grammy, taking Best Rap Album at the 2017 ceremony while Chance remained independent and kept his masters. Pitchfork scored it 9.1, and a decade on it stands as a landmark of 2010s hip-hop.

sources.title: Chance The Rapper's 'Coloring Book' Is First Streaming-Only Album To Win a Grammy

cards.hook: Suspended from high school for ten days, he wrote a mixtape instead — and named it after the punishment
cards.story: Chancelor Bennett was born in Chicago in 1993. His debut mixtape, 10 Day, took shape during a ten-day high-school suspension, turning idle time into the free release that launched his career in 2012. The following year's Acid Rap made him a national name.

cards.hook: His giddy kiss-off to record labels drafted 2 Chainz and Lil Wayne — and won a Grammy
cards.story: No Problem, a bouncing warning to any label thinking of blocking his blessings, pairs Chance with 2 Chainz and Lil Wayne. The Coloring Book standout won Best Rap Performance at the 2017 Grammy Awards, a fitting trophy for a song about staying independent.

cards.hook: After six years away from albums, he came back with a dense, Pan-Africanist second studio album
cards.story: In August 2025 Chance returned with STAR LINE, a dense, Pan-Africanist second studio album. It marked his first album since 2019's The Big Day.

media.label: Search: Chance the Rapper STAR LINE

cards.hook: Coldplay started as two guys who met during their first week of university
cards.story: Chris Martin and Jonny Buckland met during Freshers' Week at University College London in September 1996, and started writing songs together the following year. Guy Berryman and Will Champion soon joined, and by 1998 the band had settled on the name Coldplay.

cards.hook: Coldplay's last world tour became the most-attended concert tour ever — and it's on pause until 2027
cards.story: The Music of the Spheres World Tour ran from March 2022 through a paused close at Wembley Stadium in September 2025: 225 shows, 13.1 million tickets and a $1.52 billion box office, making it the most-attended tour in history. The band expects to resume it in 2027.

sources.title: Music of the Spheres World Tour

cards.hook: Coldplay built kinetic dance floors and rider-powered bikes to cut their touring emissions
cards.story: Coldplay designed the Music of the Spheres World Tour around a specific sustainability program — kinetic dance floors that generate power from crowd movement, power bikes for the audience to pedal, solar panels, and a mobile battery built from recycled BMW electric-car cells — aiming to cut the carbon footprint of stadium touring, not just play the songs at that scale.

sources.title: Music of the Spheres World Tour

cards.hook: Dave Matthews Band's original lineup had no lead guitarist — a violin and a saxophone filled the gap instead
cards.story: When Dave Matthews Band formed in Charlottesville in 1991, the founding lineup paired Dave Matthews's rhythm guitar with Boyd Tinsley's violin and LeRoi Moore's saxophone rather than a dedicated lead guitarist — an absence that became the band's defining sound rather than a gap to fill.

cards.hook: The band lost its saxophonist to a farm accident — and kept touring within days
cards.story: On June 30, 2008, LeRoi Moore was injured in an ATV accident on his farm near Charlottesville. Jeff Coffin of Béla Fleck and the Flecktones filled in for the rest of that tour, and the band played a scheduled Los Angeles show the same day Moore later died of complications from the accident. Coffin has remained the band's saxophonist since.

media.label: Dave Matthews Band live LeRoi Moore tribute

cards.hook: Dave Matthews Band entered the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2024
cards.story: After more than three decades built almost entirely on live performance and a philosophy of never playing a song the same way twice, Dave Matthews Band was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2024.

sources.title: Dave Matthews Band's Rock and Roll Hall of Fame Speech

cards.hook: Dirty Projectors made an EP with Björk — and gave the proceeds to the ocean
cards.story: In 2010 Dirty Projectors and Björk released Mount Wittenberg Orca, a collaborative EP built around the band's interlocking harmonies and Björk's voice. The money raised from the self-released digital EP was donated to the National Geographic Society's Oceans Initiatives, which helps create marine protected areas.

media.label: Dirty Projectors + Björk — Mount Wittenberg Orca

cards.hook: Bitte Orca braided three lead singers into one sound
cards.story: After Rise Above (2007) — an album of Black Flag songs re-imagined from memory — Dirty Projectors broke through with Bitte Orca in 2009. Its knotty, R&B-flecked art-rock ran on the interlocking lead vocals of Dave Longstreth, Amber Coffman and Angel Deradoorian, and drew widespread critical acclaim.

cards.hook: A witness who watched Erykah Badu's video shoot live still called the police
cards.story: For the 2010 video for 'Window Seat,' Badu stripped naked while walking through Dallas' Dealey Plaza — the site of the JFK assassination — in front of hundreds of onlookers, none of whom called police at the time. It was only after the video went viral online that a witness came forward, saying she and her two children had been offended, leading to a disorderly conduct citation. Badu pleaded not guilty before eventually paying the fine.

sources.title: Erykah Badu Pleads Not Guilty to "Window Seat" Charge

cards.hook: Erykah Badu's newest Grammy win came nearly three decades after her first
cards.story: Badu's Grammy history spans 20 nominations and 5 wins across almost 30 years — from Baduizm-era wins in the late 1990s through a 2003 Best R&B Song win for 'Love of My Life (An Ode to Hip Hop)' with Common, up to Best Melodic Rap Performance in 2025 for her feature on Rapsody's '3:AM.'

sources.title: Erykah Badu Grammy Awards and Nominations, Song & Bio

cards.hook: The night a jazz bassist beat Justin Bieber for Best New Artist
cards.story: At the 2011 Grammy Awards, Esperanza Spalding won Best New Artist, a category then dominated by pop and rock names. She became the first jazz musician to take the honor, edging out a field that included Justin Bieber, Drake, Florence + the Machine and Mumford & Sons. The upset instantly widened her audience far beyond jazz circles.

media.label: Esperanza Spalding — Black Gold (Official Music Video)

sources.title: Grammy Award for Best New Artist
GRAMMY Rewind: Esperanza Spalding Elatedly Wins Best New Artist In 2011
How Esperanza Spalding's Grammy Win Shook the Music World

cards.hook: She was teaching at Berklee at 20 — before her debut album existed
cards.story: Esperanza Spalding grew up in Portland, Oregon, and earned a scholarship to Berklee College of Music. While still barely out of her teens, she became one of the youngest instructors in the school's history. Her debut album, Junjo, arrived in 2006, opening a run of genre-crossing records.

media.label: Search: Esperanza Spalding NPR Tiny Desk Concert

cards.hook: A Brazilian legend's farewell-era duet album — with a Portland bassist
cards.story: In 2024 Spalding released Milton + esperanza, a full collaborative album with Brazilian music icon Milton Nascimento. The project pairs one of Brazil's most revered singers with Spalding's bass and voice across a set of duets and reimagined songs. It marks a career-spanning meeting between two generations of artists.

cards.hook: The Esperanza song that puts a William Blake poem to music
cards.story: On 2010's Chamber Music Society, Spalding fused a jazz trio with strings and voice. One highlight, Little Fly, sets the words of William Blake's poem to her own melody, showing how she blurs the line between song and chamber music. The album helped define the ambitious, category-defying sound of her early career.

sources.title: A Review of Esperanza Spalding's Chamber Music Society

cards.hook: A film director came calling — and an instrumental band scored Friday Night Lights without changing a thing
cards.story: After the acclaim for The Earth Is Not a Cold Dead Place, Explosions in the Sky were contacted to score the 2004 film Friday Night Lights. Despite having access to rare studio equipment for the project, the band stuck to the same wordless, guitar-built language that had always defined them — the score became inseparable from the film's identity.

media.label: Explosions in the Sky — Friday Night Lights score

sources.title: Behind the Scenes of Friday Night Lights

cards.hook: A wordless band once explained their music as love songs without the words
cards.story: Explosions in the Sky have never used vocals — three interlocking electric guitars and drums do all the talking. Guitarist Munaf Rayani has described the band's instrumentals as their version of love songs, just without any words to get in the way.

cards.hook: Sent to London to become a doctor, he enrolled in music school instead
cards.story: In 1958 Fela's family sent him to London to study medicine. He walked into Trinity College of Music instead and formed the band Koola Lobitos. Back in Lagos he fused jazz, funk, highlife and Yoruba rhythm into Afrobeat — and in 1975 he shed the surname Ransome as a colonial relic, taking Anikulapo.

cards.hook: He called soldiers zombies — and a thousand of them burned his home in reply
cards.story: On 1976's Zombie, Fela mocked the Nigerian military as mindless zombies who move only on command. The state answered in 1977: around a thousand soldiers stormed and torched his Kalakuta Republic commune. His mother, activist Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti, was thrown from a window and later died of her injuries.

cards.hook: He delivered his mother's coffin to the head of state's barracks — then made a record of it
cards.story: After the Kalakuta raid killed his mother, Fela led a march that carried her coffin to Dodan Barracks, the seat of Nigeria's military government. He turned the protest into the searing 1981 release Coffin for Head of State, aiming his grief straight at the top.

media.label: Fela Kuti — Coffin for Head of State

sources.title: Fela's stories: Coffin for Head of State

cards.hook: Fela's life became a Broadway musical, carrying Afrobeat from Lagos to the American stage
cards.story: Fela!, a stage musical built around his music and story, opened on Broadway in 2009, carrying Afrobeat from Lagos to the heart of American theater.

cards.hook: After 40-plus years in Red Hot Chili Peppers, Flea finally made a solo album — on trumpet
cards.story: Flea co-founded Red Hot Chili Peppers in 1983, but it took until 2026 for him to release a debut album under his own name. Honora, out via Nonesuch Records, is built around the instrument he played before he ever picked up a bass: he plays trumpet and bass across the record.

sources.title: Flea Jazzes Up for Debut Solo Album 'Honora'

cards.hook: Flea's solo debut pulled in Thom Yorke and Nick Cave
cards.story: Honora is a covers-and-originals record with a striking guest list. Thom Yorke and Nick Cave both contribute vocals, and the single 'Traffic Lights' was co-written with Yorke — the loudest bassist in rock trading the arena for something hushed and collaborative.

sources.title: Flea Jazzes Up for Debut Solo Album 'Honora'

cards.hook: The Chili Peppers bassist co-founded a nonprofit music school for kids
cards.story: In 2001, Flea and Chili Peppers collaborator Keith 'Tree' Barry opened the Silverlake Conservatory of Music, a nonprofit community music school in Los Angeles. It has become one of his life's largest projects — a school built to keep music education alive for kids in the city he came up in.

cards.hook: Flogging Molly's whole sound was born from a Monday-night pub residency
cards.story: Dave King, previously the frontman of the hard rock band Fastway, started playing Irish traditional music mixed with rock at a weekly residency at Molly Malone's, an LA pub. The band built its earliest songs and its following there — the pub gave Flogging Molly both its name and its entire musical premise.

cards.hook: Flogging Molly run their own label — and their own cruise
cards.story: Rather than stay tied to outside labels, Flogging Molly release music through Borstal Beat Records, their own imprint. They've built the same independence into their live world: the band's own annual Salty Dog Cruise has sailed musicians and fans together since 2015.

cards.hook: The band's name is two studio nicknames stitched into one
cards.story: Florence + the Machine came together in London in 2007 around singer Florence Welch and keyboardist Isabella Summers. Their early collaborations went under the playful handle Florence Robot / Isa Machine, and when that got shortened it became the name that stuck. Welch's huge, theatrical voice quickly marked the project out from the late-2000s indie pack.

cards.hook: Their debut needed over six months to finally hit No.1
cards.story: Lungs arrived in July 2009 and became one of the defining British debuts of its era, but its climb to the summit was a slow burn. The album didn't reach number one on the UK Albums Chart until January 2010, powered along by singles like Dog Days Are Over and the band's cover of You've Got the Love.

sources.title: 'Lungs': How Florence + The Machine Breathed New Life Into Pop

cards.hook: The follow-up went bigger, darker — harps, choirs and a No.1
cards.story: Ceremonials, released in 2011, traded the debut's scrappiness for chamber-pop grandeur: harps, choirs and cathedral-sized arrangements. It topped the UK Albums Chart and gave the band the anthem Shake It Out, one of their most enduring live moments.

sources.title: Shake It Out (Florence and the Machine song)

cards.hook: Their sixth album came out of a life-threatening scare on tour
cards.story: In 2025 Florence + the Machine released Everybody Scream, their sixth studio album. It was written in the wake of a serious, life-threatening health emergency Florence Welch experienced while touring, and it landed to wide critical acclaim. Nearly two decades in, the band spent summer 2026 headlining festivals across Europe.

media.label: Search: Florence + the Machine Everybody Scream

cards.hook: Before Fred again.., an apprenticeship under Brian Eno
cards.story: Frederick Gibson grew up in London and came up under the mentorship of Brian Eno, beginning years of behind-the-scenes work as a producer and songwriter before he stepped out front as Fred again..

cards.hook: A lockdown anthem about the thing everyone had lost: dancing
cards.story: 'Marea (We've Lost Dancing)' pairs Fred again.. with The Blessed Madonna, turning a spoken reflection on pandemic isolation into a euphoric release. It became one of his defining early singles as a solo artist. Its title names exactly what clubs and crowds were missing in 2020–2021.

media.label: Marea (We've Lost Dancing) feat. The Blessed Madonna

cards.hook: Two Grammys in one night — for an album and a track built from friends' voices
cards.story: At the 2024 Grammy Awards, Fred again..'s Actual Life 3 won Best Dance/Electronic Album, while 'Rumble' — his collaboration with Skrillex and Flowdan — took Best Dance/Electronic Recording. It capped a rapid rise from behind-the-desk producer to headliner. Both wins came from work rooted in his diary-entry approach to dance music.

media.label: Search: Rumble — Fred again.., Skrillex, Flowdan

cards.hook: July 2022: a Boiler Room set in London became a word-of-mouth landmark of his rise
cards.story: In July 2022 Fred again.. played a Boiler Room set in London that spread far beyond the room, becoming one of the most-watched broadcasts in the series' history. Momentum carried into a sold-out Madison Square Garden show alongside Skrillex and Four Tet in February 2023, then a Coachella closing set that April with the same trio. The stretch marked one of the steepest ascents in recent dance music.

media.label: Search: Fred again.. Boiler Room London 2022

sources.title: Boiler Room: Fred again.. in London, Jul 29, 2022 (DJ Mix)

cards.hook: A Blur frontman and a Tank Girl cartoonist built pop's longest-running fake band
cards.story: Gorillaz began in 1998 as a collaboration between Blur's Damon Albarn and artist Jamie Hewlett, who created a band fronted by four cartoon characters — 2-D, Murdoc Niccals, Noodle and Russel Hobbs — with real musicians working behind the animation. The concept caught on so widely that Guinness World Records recognized Gorillaz as the most successful virtual band. Nearly three decades later, Hewlett's characters keep evolving alongside the music.

cards.hook: The Grammy-winning song with De La Soul that became Gorillaz' signature track
cards.story: Feel Good Inc. is the standout single from Demon Days (2005), the album Gorillaz made with producer Danger Mouse. Built around a cackling verse from hip-hop group De La Soul, it won a Grammy for Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals. Two decades on it remains the band's defining song.

cards.hook: The breakthrough hit built on a rapper's rhymes and a gospel choir refrain
cards.story: Clint Eastwood launched Gorillaz from their 2001 debut album, pairing Damon Albarn's woozy melody with a rap verse from Del the Funky Homosapien. The song's success turned the cartoon experiment into a genuine pop force. Its haunting refrain and animated video remain the band's calling card.

cards.hook: Gorillaz recorded part of their ninth album in India — and it hit UK number one
cards.story: In February 2026 Gorillaz released The Mountain on their own Kong label, a meditation on grief and the afterlife recorded partly in India and sung in multiple languages. It became the band's third UK number-one album. The record continued Gorillaz' long habit of reinventing itself with each phase.

sources.title: Gorillaz plant Number 1 flag atop The Mountain

cards.hook: Their breakout single made them the first Australian act nominated in a Grammy R&B category
cards.story: Hiatus Kaiyote came together in Melbourne in 2011, and by their debut album Tawk Tomahawk they'd caught the ear of the wider soul world. The single Nakamarra, featuring a guest verse from A Tribe Called Quest's Q-Tip, earned a nomination for Best R&B Performance at the 2014 Grammys — a first for an Australian act in an R&B category.

sources.title: Hiatus Kaiyote Nominated For A Grammy
Melbourne's Hiatus Kaiyote 'Completely Floored' By Grammys Nomination

cards.hook: Drake, Beyoncé and JAY-Z have all built tracks around Hiatus Kaiyote samples
cards.story: For a band from Melbourne, their fingerprints turn up in some huge places. Drake sampled their song Building a Ladder on Free Smoke, the opener of his 2017 project More Life. And Beyoncé and JAY-Z, performing as the Carters, used Hiatus Kaiyote's music on their 2018 album Everything Is Love — proof of how far their future-soul reached.

sources.title: Drake's 'More Life': How His Love of Hiatus Kaiyote Turned Into a Sample
Everything Is Love (sample credits: '713' samples 'The World It Softly Lulls')

cards.hook: For Mood Valiant they linked up with a cult Brazilian arranger from the 1970s
cards.story: Mood Valiant, released in 2021, marked the band's return after a long gap. Its song Get Sun features a lush string arrangement from Arthur Verocai, the Brazilian composer whose rare 1972 self-titled album became a crate-digger legend. The album went on to earn Hiatus Kaiyote a Grammy nomination for Best Progressive R&B Album.

media.label: Search: Hiatus Kaiyote Get Sun Arthur Verocai

sources.title: Hiatus Kaiyote Preview New Album With 'Get Sun'

cards.hook: Their fourth album arrived in June 2024 — and it opens a new chapter
cards.story: Love Heart Cheat Code, the band's fourth studio album, arrived in June 2024. It's a tighter, punchier record than the sprawling works before it, and led into a run of tours across North America, Europe, Japan and Australia. The title track and Make Friends show off the band's knack for melody wrapped in polyrhythm.

cards.hook: Hozier is only the fourth Irish act ever to top the Billboard Hot 100
cards.story: 'Too Sweet' reached No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 2024, surpassing the No. 2 peak of 'Take Me to Church' a decade earlier and giving Hozier his first US chart-topper. It made him the first Irish act to top the Hot 100 since Sinéad O'Connor's 'Nothing Compares 2 U' in 1990 — and only the fourth ever, alongside O'Connor, Gilbert O'Sullivan and U2.

cards.hook: 'Too Sweet' broke a chart-longevity record on its way to becoming a hit
cards.story: In October 2025, 'Too Sweet' broke the record for most weeks ever on the American Top 40 chart, reaching its 76th week on the countdown. 'Take Me to Church', Hozier's earlier breakthrough, had already been certified Diamond in the US, Australia, Canada and France.

cards.hook: Hozier built a whole album around Dante's nine circles of Hell
cards.story: Unreal Unearth (2023) is structured loosely around Dante Alighieri's Inferno, its songs mapped onto the nine circles of Hell as a framework for working through pandemic-era isolation — a genuinely documented concept, not a marketing gloss, confirmed in Hozier's own words to the Recording Academy.

sources.title: Hozier on Unreal Unearth, Dante's Inferno and His Pandemic Inspirations

cards.hook: A dubstep superstar signed a hushed folk-electronic band that sounded like nothing else on his label
cards.story: Hundred Waters came together in Gainesville, Florida, in 2011 around Nicole Miglis, Trayer Tryon, and Zach Tetreault, weaving folk instruments into electronic textures. Their delicate sound caught the ear of Skrillex, whose label OWSLA signed them despite the roster leaning toward heavier electronic music. It remains one of the more unexpected pairings in the label's history.

media.label: Hundred Waters — Show Me Love (Official Video)

sources.title: Hundred Waters and Skrillex's New Team-Up Will Make You Feel Better

cards.hook: Their spare, haunted second album earned Pitchfork's Best New Music badge
cards.story: The Moon Rang Like a Bell, released in 2014, distilled Hundred Waters into something stark and spectral — Nicole Miglis' voice ringing over sparse electronics. Pitchfork gave the record its Best New Music designation. Tracks like Cavity and Murmurs became touchstones of the band's sound.

sources.title: Hundred Waters on Life After Pitchfork's 'Best New Music' Tag

cards.hook: They didn't just make records — they built a festival in a utopian desert town
cards.story: In 2014 Hundred Waters helped launch FORM Arcosanti, an intimate, application-driven festival. It's held at Arcosanti, an experimental town in the Arizona high desert designed by architect Paolo Soleri. The gathering grew into one of the most beloved small festivals in American music.

media.label: Search: Hundred Waters at FORM Arcosanti

sources.title: Meet FORM: Arcosanti, The Tiny Desert Festival With A Big Dream

cards.hook: An a cappella song built entirely from her own voice — then it powered Jason Derulo's Whatcha Say
cards.story: Imogen Heap closed Speak for Yourself with Hide and Seek, a track made almost entirely from her voice run through a vocal harmonizer. It reached a huge audience after appearing in a season-two finale of The O.C., and years later its melody became the backbone of Jason Derulo's 2009 single Whatcha Say, introducing Heap's sound to a new generation.

media.label: Imogen Heap — Hide and Seek (official video)

cards.hook: She won a Grammy for engineering — an award she earned on her own record
cards.story: After self-funding Speak for Yourself, Heap followed it with Ellipse in 2009, writing, performing, producing and engineering it herself. Her hands-on studio work earned her the Grammy Award for Best Engineered Album, Non-Classical — a category more often won by dedicated engineers than by the artist behind the songs.

cards.hook: Before going solo she was half of Frou Frou — and a Garden State scene made them a cult favorite
cards.story: Frou Frou paired Imogen Heap with producer Guy Sigsworth, releasing the album Details in 2002. The duo went quiet, but their song Let Go found a second life on the soundtrack to Zach Braff's 2004 film Garden State, helping turn Details into a word-of-mouth cult classic just as Heap's solo career took off.

cards.hook: She helped build gloves that let you sculpt music with your hands
cards.story: Heap has long worked at the crossroads of music and technology, co-developing the Mi.Mu gloves — wearable controllers that translate hand movements and gestures into live sound. She has used them on stage to shape her performances, part of a career spent building her own tools alongside her records.

media.label: Search: Imogen Heap Mi.Mu gloves performance

cards.hook: Iron and Wine was discovered through a mix CD passed hand to hand
cards.story: Before Iron and Wine existed as a project, Sam Beam was a Florida State University film student recording late-night songs at home with future sound designer EJ Holowicki. One of those recordings was shared, untitled, with childhood friend Ben Bridwell (later of Band of Horses), who passed it around on mix CDs during what Sub Pop calls 'the golden era of mix CDs' — eventually reaching Sub Pop, who signed Beam and released those home recordings almost as they were.

sources.title: Archive Series Volume No. 5: Tallahassee Recordings

cards.hook: A movie soundtrack, not radio, built Iron and Wine's early audience
cards.story: Iron and Wine's cover of The Postal Service's 'Such Great Heights' appeared on the 2004 Garden State soundtrack. In Sam Beam's own words: 'It was night and day. You'd be playing to 200 people, and then all of a sudden you're playing to 2,000' — though he notes the biggest jump actually came a few years later, after a song appeared on the Twilight soundtrack.

sources.title: "You Gotta Hear This One Song": The Oral History of the Garden State Soundtrack

cards.hook: A Stevie Wonder cover on YouTube got Jacob Collier discovered by Quincy Jones himself
cards.story: Jacob Collier's early homemade videos — split-screen arrangements where he sang and played every part himself — included a cover of Stevie Wonder's 'Don't You Worry 'Bout a Thing' that drew millions of views. It caught the attention of Quincy Jones, who flew Collier to the Montreux Jazz Festival and introduced him to Herbie Hancock.

sources.title: With 'In My Room,' Jazz Phenom Jacob Collier Is Bringing Jubilation Back

cards.hook: Jacob Collier has 7 Grammys — almost all for the same rare skill: arranging
cards.story: Jacob Collier has won 7 Grammy Awards from 16 nominations, the vast majority in the arranging categories — Best Arrangement, Instruments and Vocals and Best Arrangement, Instrumental or A Cappella. It's a level of recognition in that specific, technical corner of music that almost no one his age has matched.

media.label: All I Need (with Mahalia & Ty Dolla $ign)

sources.title: Jacob Collier — Grammy Awards artist page

cards.hook: He chopped R&B into ghosts, then put his own hymnal voice at the center
cards.story: James Blake surfaced from London's post-dubstep world in 2010 with EPs like CMYK, splicing soul vocals over cavernous sub-bass. A year later his self-titled debut flipped the script, foregrounding his fragile voice in skeletal arrangements. The album's cover of Feist's Limit to Your Love became his unlikely calling card.

cards.hook: The album that won the Mercury Prize — and gave him the song he's most known for
cards.story: Overgrown arrived in 2013 and took the Mercury Prize that year, the UK's marquee album award. Its lead single, Retrograde, remains James Blake's best-known song, a slow build from hush to a synth-heavy swell. It's the record that turned a cult electronic figure into a fixture of modern music.

cards.hook: His fingerprints are on Beyoncé's Lemonade and Kendrick's DAMN.
cards.story: Beyond his own records, James Blake became a go-to voice and producer across modern pop and hip-hop. He duets with Beyoncé on Forward from Lemonade, and holds production credits on Kendrick Lamar's DAMN. The collaboration web stretches wide — a quiet studio presence behind some of the era's biggest albums.

cards.hook: He made a whole album with Lil Yachty — and called it Bad Cameo
cards.story: In 2024 James Blake paired with rapper Lil Yachty for Bad Cameo, a full collaborative album rather than a one-off guest spot. It's one of the more unexpected team-ups in his catalog, matching his sub-bass melancholy with Yachty's genre-restless streak. The record underscores how far his collaborative instincts reach.

cards.hook: 'I'm Yours' broke a chart record it took over a decade to lose
cards.story: Jason Mraz's 'I'm Yours' peaked at only No. 6 on the Billboard Hot 100 — but it spent 76 weeks on the chart, breaking the previous record for longest-charting Hot 100 song, LeAnn Rimes' 'How Do I Live' (69 weeks). The record stood until Teddy Swims' 'Lose Control' passed it with 100 weeks.

cards.hook: Jason Mraz won a Grammy for a song he wrote with Colbie Caillat
cards.story: In 2010, Mraz and Colbie Caillat won the Grammy Award for Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals for 'Lucky', a duet the two co-wrote together. Away from music, Mraz runs a working organic avocado farm in Oceanside, California — Mraz Family Farms — not a side hobby but a real operating orchard.

cards.hook: Jeff Buckley's 'Hallelujah' hit No. 1 eleven years after his death
cards.story: Buckley's reading of Leonard Cohen's 'Hallelujah' — learned from John Cale's recording on a Cohen tribute album — was never a hit in his lifetime. In March 2008, after a performance of the song on American Idol sent listeners searching, his version debuted at No. 1 on Billboard's Hot Digital Songs chart: his first No. 1 on any Billboard chart, eleven years after he died. In 2014 the recording entered the Library of Congress's National Recording Registry.

cards.hook: Grace climbed 156 places on Rolling Stone's greatest-albums list — decades after release
cards.story: Grace (1994) was the only studio album Jeff Buckley completed, and it wasn't a blockbuster on arrival. Its stature has only grown: Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Albums list ranked it No. 303 in 2003, and the 2020 revision moved it up to No. 147. In 2026 Buckley received his first Rock and Roll Hall of Fame nomination.

sources.title: Grace — The 500 Greatest Albums of All Time

cards.hook: A tiny café residency became one of the most famous live records of the '90s
cards.story: Before Grace, Buckley spent 1993 playing Monday nights at Sin-é, a small East Village café — just voice and electric guitar, covers and works in progress. Columbia signed him out of that room, and the recordings became Live at Sin-É. His last performance was at Barristers in Memphis on May 26, 1997, three days before he drowned in the Wolf River Harbor.

cards.hook: Before she made records, Jesca Hoop was Tom Waits' nanny — and he became her first champion
cards.story: Born in Santa Rosa and raised in a Mormon family where harmony singing was daily practice, Jesca Hoop drifted through the American West before landing in Los Angeles. There she took a job caring for Tom Waits and Kathleen Brennan's children, and Waits ended up championing her early demos. Her debut album Kismet arrived in 2007, launching a catalog built on intricate fingerstyle guitar and dreamlike imagery.

cards.hook: She and Iron & Wine's Sam Beam wrote a whole album of duets together
cards.story: In 2016 Jesca Hoop teamed with Sam Beam, the songwriter behind Iron & Wine, for Love Letter for Fire — an album of duets the two wrote together. It paired her unpredictable melodies with his hushed folk sensibility, a meeting of two distinctive American songwriters. The collaboration expanded a network that would later include John Parish and the duo Lucius.

media.label: Search: Love Letter for Fire live

cards.hook: For Stonechild she brought in a PJ Harvey collaborator and the duo Lucius
cards.story: Jesca Hoop's 2019 album Stonechild was produced by John Parish, long a key collaborator of PJ Harvey. The record also features guest vocals from Lucius, the harmony-driven duo, most prominently on Shoulder Charge. The pairing of Parish's textured production with Hoop's stark songwriting drew wide critical praise.

cards.hook: Her stark nine-song Sub Pop debut won over NPR for its refusal to be predictable
cards.story: After years on independent labels, Jesca Hoop signed to Sub Pop and released Memories Are Now in 2017. The pared-back, nine-song set leaned on her voice and guitar, earning strong reviews for its willingness to surprise. It cemented her reputation as one of modern songwriting's best-kept secrets.

cards.hook: One album, two legends of the studio: Albini recorded it and Van Dyke Parks scored it
cards.story: For Ys, her 2006 second album, Joanna Newsom assembled an extraordinary crew. Steve Albini engineered the recordings and Van Dyke Parks wrote the sweeping orchestral arrangements. The result was just five long-form songs, some stretching past ten minutes, built around harp and voice.

cards.hook: Paul Thomas Anderson cast her as the narrator of a whole feature film
cards.story: The bond between Joanna Newsom and filmmaker Paul Thomas Anderson is real: she narrates and appears in his film Inherent Vice. Sapokanikan and Divers, meanwhile, are two songs from her 2015 album Divers.

cards.hook: A harp-playing kid whose 2004 debut helped define a whole folk movement
cards.story: Joanna Newsom began studying harp as a child. In 2004 she released her debut, The Milk-Eyed Mender, on Chicago label Drag City — an album of harp, harpsichord and a striking, keening voice that made her one of the defining figures of the era's new folk wave.

cards.hook: She dismissed Spotify as a villainous cabal — and kept her whole catalog off it for years
cards.story: Joanna Newsom is famously deliberate about how her music reaches listeners. She long refused to put her catalog on Spotify, memorably dismissing the service as a villainous cabal of major labels, and for years her albums lived only on physical formats and downloads. Today her records stream and sell through her official Bandcamp page, run with Drag City.

sources.title: Joanna Newsom slams Spotify as 'built from the ground up to circumvent the idea of paying artists'
Joanna Newsom official Bandcamp page (full catalog, Drag City)

cards.hook: In a single year, John Frusciante released six solo albums
cards.story: In 2004, away from Red Hot Chili Peppers, Frusciante went on a tear: six solo albums in one year, each chasing a different genre and recording method. The run included Shadows Collide With People — his first solo record to reach a chart — alongside The Will to Death, Inside of Emptiness, A Sphere in the Heart of Silence and Curtains.

cards.hook: The Chili Peppers guitarist quietly became an acid-house producer named Trickfinger
cards.story: Better known for melodic guitar, Frusciante spent the 2010s teaching himself hardware synthesis and drum programming, releasing acid-house and electronic records under the alias Trickfinger. In 2020 he brought that world back to his own name with Maya, an instrumental electronic album.

cards.hook: Radiohead's guitarist has three Oscar nominations — for film scores
cards.story: Alongside his day jobs in Radiohead and The Smile, Jonny Greenwood has become one of cinema's most sought-after composers. He has been nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Score three times: for Phantom Thread, The Power of the Dog and One Battle After Another.

sources.title: List of awards and nominations received by Jonny Greenwood
Why Jonny Greenwood Deserves to Win the Oscar for 'One Battle After Another'

cards.hook: Greenwood has scored every Paul Thomas Anderson film since There Will Be Blood
cards.story: The partnership began with There Will Be Blood in 2007 and never stopped: Greenwood went on to score The Master, Inherent Vice, Phantom Thread, Licorice Pizza and One Battle After Another for director Paul Thomas Anderson — one of the longest-running composer-director teams in modern film.

cards.hook: One of the most acclaimed scores of the 2000s was ruled ineligible for an Oscar
cards.story: Greenwood's There Will Be Blood score was widely tipped for the Academy Award for Best Original Score — but it was ruled ineligible because it drew on pre-existing music, including his own concert piece Popcorn Superhet Receiver. The rules disqualified exactly what made the score singular.

media.label: There Will Be Blood — Jonny Greenwood

cards.hook: Khruangbin is named after the Thai word for airplane
cards.story: Bassist Laura Lee named the band while she was learning Thai — 'Khruangbin' translates to 'airplane' or 'engine fly,' a nod to the international sweep of Thai funk, dub, psych-rock and soul the trio pulls from. The name has turned out fitting for a band that built its career on nearly constant touring.

sources.title: On The Cover – Khruangbin: "We planned our entire world tour around Glastonbury"

cards.hook: Khruangbin's wigs are a persona Laura Lee can leave on stage
cards.story: Onstage, Laura Lee and Mark Speer perform behind black wigs. In her own words: 'I definitely feel like a character when I'm on stage and wearing the wig... Having a costume helped me think that I was bigger than I am, because I was quite a nervous player at first. And now it protects me in a different way — it's protecting my privacy.' The band didn't anticipate how much significance the costumes would take on when they started wearing them.

sources.title: On The Cover – Khruangbin: "We planned our entire world tour around Glastonbury"

cards.hook: Led Zeppelin's biggest album has no title and no band name on the cover
cards.story: After lukewarm reviews for Led Zeppelin III, the band's fourth album carried no title, no artist name and nothing on the cover but four symbols — one chosen by each member. It became one of the best-selling albums in history, with an estimated 37 million copies sold, and carried 'Black Dog', 'Rock and Roll' and 'Stairway to Heaven'.

cards.hook: Led Zeppelin's fullest reunion came 27 years after they disbanded — with the drummer's son on drums
cards.story: John Bonham died on September 25, 1980, and the surviving members disbanded rather than continue without him. They reunited only rarely — most fully at the 2007 Ahmet Ertegun Tribute Concert at London's O2 Arena, with Jason Bonham, John's son, on drums. The show was later released as the film and album Celebration Day.

media.label: Led Zeppelin — Ahmet Ertegun Tribute Concert 2007

cards.hook: Led Zeppelin entered the Rock Hall in 1995 and got a Grammy for their whole career a decade later
cards.story: Led Zeppelin were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1995, and in 2005 the Recording Academy gave them the Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award. Between the two, an estimated 200 to 300 million records sold worldwide place them among the best-selling music artists of all time.

sources.title: Led Zeppelin — Rock & Roll Hall of Fame

cards.hook: An American moved to Iceland and turned the loneliness into a band
cards.story: Ryan Karazija had fronted the Oakland indie band Audrye Sessions when he relocated to Reykjavík, Iceland — and out of that displacement founded Low Roar, whose self-titled 2011 debut turned homesickness and distance into hushed, glacial electronic folk.

sources.title: Obituary: Death Stranding musician Ryan Karazija has passed away at 40

cards.hook: Low Roar's songs became the emotional core of Death Stranding
cards.story: Low Roar's music was woven through Hideo Kojima's 2019 game Death Stranding — the track 'I'll Keep Coming' had already scored the game's first reveal trailer — and it is how the band reached a global audience. Ryan Karazija died in October 2022 at the age of 40.

media.label: Low Roar — I'll Keep Coming (Death Stranding)

sources.title: Obituary: Death Stranding musician Ryan Karazija has passed away at 40
Death Stranding composer Ryan Karazija dies at 40

cards.hook: Asked to play 'unplugged' on TV, a punk band showed up as a mariachi ensemble instead
cards.story: In 2006, LA punk band The Bronx were booked to play an acoustic version of one of their songs for television. Rather than do the soft-strummed 'unplugged' cliché, they arranged it as mariachi — and the disguise became a real band. Mariachi El Bronx has now released four albums of sincere, fully-costumed mariachi, the latest in February 2026.

sources.title: Mariachi Punk: The Bronx at Home in L.A.

cards.hook: The Killers put a mariachi punk band on their Christmas single
cards.story: In 2009, The Killers released the charity single '¡Happy Birthday Guadalupe!' featuring Wild Light and Mariachi El Bronx — trumpets, vihuela and all. It stands as one of the stranger and warmer crossovers of the era: an arena-rock band handing its holiday song to punks in charro suits.

sources.title: Mariachi El Bronx on World Cafe

cards.hook: A brother's death and a shelved album drove him out of rap — he returned in a metal mask
cards.story: Daniel Dumile first broke through as Zev Love X of the group KMD. When his brother and bandmate DJ Subroc died in 1993 and their label shelved the album Black Bastards, he vanished from music entirely. He resurfaced in the late 1990s with his face hidden behind a metal mask modeled on Marvel's Doctor Doom — reborn as MF DOOM.

sources.title: MF DOOM, Intricate And Enigmatic Rapper And Producer, Dead At 49

cards.hook: An unfinished version leaked online in 2002 — so DOOM and Madlib rebuilt it into Madvillainy
cards.story: Madvillainy paired MF DOOM's dense internal rhyme with producer Madlib's crate-dug beats. When early demos leaked in 2002, the pair regrouped and reworked the record before its 2004 release on Stones Throw. It became one of the most acclaimed underground rap albums ever made.

cards.hook: His food-themed classic MM..FOOD is a scrambled version of his own name
cards.story: Released in 2004, MM..FOOD leaned into DOOM's obsession with cartoons, comics and cuisine, with tracks built around eating and villainy. The title itself is an anagram of MF DOOM. Rapp Snitch Knishes, its standout featuring Mr. Fantastik, became one of his best-loved songs.

cards.hook: He died on Halloween 2020 — but the world didn't learn of it until New Year's Eve
cards.story: Born in the UK and never a US citizen, DOOM was denied re-entry to the United States after a 2010 tour and spent his final decade in England. He died on October 31, 2020, at age 49. His family did not announce the news until December 31, 2020, keeping his passing hidden for two months.

sources.title: MF Doom's Wife Reveals Rapper's Cause of Death

cards.hook: The album so big it made Michael Jackson the first artist to send five singles to #1
cards.story: After Thriller, the question was how to possibly follow it. Bad (1987) answered by becoming the first album to spawn five Billboard Hot 100 number-one singles. It cemented Jackson's dominance of late-'80s pop.

cards.hook: The best-selling album in history turned three songs into short films that changed pop
cards.story: Thriller (1982) remains the best-selling album ever released. Its short films for Billie Jean, Beat It, and the title track were among the works that helped turn the music video into a cultural event rather than mere promotion.

cards.hook: One TV performance in 1983 made the moonwalk a national obsession overnight
cards.story: At the Motown 25 television special, Jackson performed Billie Jean and glided backward across the stage in what became one of television's most replayed moments. The moonwalk, which he'd been developing and adapting, became instantly identified with him after that night.

media.label: Michael Jackson Motown 25 Billie Jean performance

cards.hook: Before Thriller, a disco-jazz record with Quincy Jones set the whole thing in motion
cards.story: Off the Wall (1979) was Jackson's first solo album made with producer Quincy Jones, blending disco energy with a jazz sensibility. It launched the adult solo career that Thriller and Bad would build upon, and remains one of pop's most joyful records.

cards.hook: Modest Mouse's Isaac Brock signed Mimicking Birds — and recorded them in his house
cards.story: Mimicking Birds began as Nate Lacy's home-recording project before Isaac Brock of Modest Mouse signed it to his label, Glacial Pace Recordings, in 2008. The self-titled 2010 debut was largely recorded at Brock's Portland home, and the project grew into a trio — quiet, looping songs about deep time that found their audience through the Modest Mouse family tree.

cards.hook: Seven years of silence, then a new Mimicking Birds song appeared
cards.story: Mimicking Birds last toured in 2018 behind Layers of Us, and then went quiet — no shows, an expired website, the catalog resting on Bandcamp. In October 2025 a new self-released single, 'Crowns', surfaced without fanfare: proof the project is still breathing, on its own unhurried clock.

cards.hook: Before Float On, they turned dead-end highways and desert sprawl into indie-rock scripture
cards.story: Modest Mouse came together in Issaquah, Washington, in the early 1990s around Isaac Brock, Jeremiah Green, and Eric Judy. Their 1997 record The Lonesome Crowded West mapped strip malls, drifters, and existential dread onto sprawling guitar epics, and it became one of the most influential indie albums of its decade long before the band saw the mainstream.

cards.hook: The reassuring singalong that turned an underground band into unlikely Grammy nominees
cards.story: Float On was the lead single from 2004's Good News for People Who Love Bad News, and its buoyant refrain pushed Modest Mouse from cult favorites into the mainstream. The song earned Grammy nominations and helped make the album a commercial breakthrough for a band that had spent years playing sprawling, jagged epics.

sources.title: Good News for People Who Love Bad News

cards.hook: They recruited a former Smiths guitarist — and landed a number-one album
cards.story: For 2007's We Were Dead Before the Ship Even Sank, Modest Mouse brought in Johnny Marr, the guitarist who had defined the sound of The Smiths in the 1980s. The collaboration paid off commercially: the album debuted at number one on the Billboard 200, the band's biggest chart peak.

sources.title: We Were Dead Before the Ship Even Sank
Modest Mouse Steers Its 'Ship' To No. 1 Debut

cards.hook: Their first fully independent album since the '90s — and the first without founding drummer Jeremiah Green
cards.story: Jeremiah Green, Modest Mouse's founding drummer, died of cancer at the end of 2022. In June 2026 the band released An Eraser and a Maze on Isaac Brock's own Glacial Pace label, their first fully independent record since the 1990s and the first made without Green, and spent the summer touring North America.

media.label: Modest Mouse — Lampshades on Fire (official video)

cards.hook: The genre-defying singer turned up acting in The Weeknd's HBO show
cards.story: Beyond the music, Moses Sumney has moved into screen work — in 2023 he appeared in The Idol, the HBO series co-created by The Weeknd. It is of a piece with a career that has always spilled past the edges of 'musician'.

cards.hook: Two albums, no fixed genre: Aromanticism and the two-part græ
cards.story: Moses Sumney's debut Aromanticism (2017), on Jagjaguwar, framed an entire record around the absence of romantic love. His second album, græ (2020), arrived in two parts — a sprawling statement about refusing to be pinned to any single category, sound or self.

cards.hook: Island Records signed him while he was still a Cambridge student
cards.story: Nick Drake grew up in the Warwickshire village of Tanworth-in-Arden and was discovered as a student at Cambridge, leading to a deal with Island Records. His 1969 debut, Five Leaves Left, paired his hushed, harmonically intricate songs with producer Joe Boyd and the string arrangements of his friend Robert Kirby. It sold poorly at the time — a fate all three of his albums shared — yet it now sits among the most admired English folk records of its era.

cards.hook: His final album is almost all voice and guitar — cut in two late-night sessions
cards.story: For Pink Moon, Nick Drake stripped his sound to the bone. Recorded with engineer John Wood in a pair of late-night sessions in October 1971, the album is just voice and acoustic guitar, save for a single piano overdub on the title track. Released in February 1972, it barely registered — but its stark intimacy is now central to why listeners keep finding him.

cards.hook: A 1999 car advert introduced Pink Moon to millions who'd never heard him
cards.story: Decades after his death, a Volkswagen commercial did what his lifetime never could. The 1999 ad used the title track of Pink Moon, and the exposure introduced Drake to a mass audience that had never heard him — helping reverse years of neglect. It remains one of the most cited examples of a commercial reviving an overlooked artist.

cards.hook: A new generation lined up to cover the songs almost nobody bought when he was alive
cards.story: Contemporary artists have reinterpreted Nick Drake's catalogue. Feist reworked River Man, while Christian Lee Hutson and Elanor Moss took on Which Will — both accompanied by official films. It's a vivid measure of how completely the verdict on Drake has reversed since his death in 1974.

media.label: River Man — Famous Blue Cable feat. Feist (Official Film)
Which Will — Christian Lee Hutson & Elanor Moss (Official Film)

sources.title: The Endless Coloured Ways: The Songs of Nick Drake

cards.hook: They left the industry, built their own label — and sold 100,000 records anyway
cards.story: After friction with the music industry over their debut, Barcelona's Ojos de Brujo founded their own label, La Fábrica de Colores, and self-released Barí (2002). The album — flamenco palmas and rumba catalana wired through hip-hop turntablism, a sound they called 'jipjop flamenkillo' — sold over 100,000 copies with no major label behind it.

sources.title: Ojos de Brujo: Setting Sights on America

cards.hook: A self-run Barcelona collective won a Latin Grammy — and a BBC world-music prize
cards.story: Ojos de Brujo took the Europe award at the BBC Radio 3 Awards for World Music in 2004, and in 2007 their album Techarí won the Latin Grammy for Best Flamenco Album — institutional recognition for a collective that ran its own label and answered to nobody. They said farewell in 2011 and played their final shows in 2012, leaving one of the most distinctive catalogs in modern flamenco.

sources.title: Latin Grammy Award for Best Flamenco Album
BBC Radio 3 Awards for World Music

cards.hook: At barely 21 she cut a debut about polygamy and arranged marriage — and it sold hundreds of thousands
cards.story: Raised in Bamako, Oumou Sangaré made her debut with Moussolou. Its songs confronted polygamy, arranged marriage and the constraints on women's lives with startling directness, and it became a phenomenon across West Africa. The record introduced a voice that would soon travel far beyond Mali.

cards.hook: For her boldest album she paired Wassoulou tradition with the drummer who helped invent Afrobeat
cards.story: On Mogoya, Oumou Sangaré folded her Wassoulou roots into electronics and modern production, working with the French collective A.R.T. The album features Tony Allen, the Nigerian drummer long associated with Fela Kuti and the birth of Afrobeat. The result was widely seen as a striking reinvention of her sound.

cards.hook: She has a Grammy on her shelf — earned by singing on a John Lennon song with Herbie Hancock
cards.story: Oumou Sangaré's album Seya earned a Grammy nomination, marking her arrival on the awards' radar. She also appeared on the title track of Herbie Hancock's The Imagine Project, a version of John Lennon's Imagine. That recording won Best Pop Collaboration with Vocals at the 2011 Grammy Awards, giving Sangaré a share of the prize.

cards.hook: After going electric, she came back to the acoustic sound — and critics called Timbuktu her finest late-career work
cards.story: With Timbuktu, released in 2022, Oumou Sangaré returned to warmer, largely acoustic textures after the electronic experiments of Mogoya. Named for the fabled Malian city, the album drew wide critical acclaim and reaffirmed her standing as one of Africa's most commanding voices. It showed an artist still evolving decades after her debut.

cards.hook: Perfume Genius wrote an entire album as the score for a modern-dance piece
cards.story: Ugly Season (2022) did not begin as a set of songs. Mike Hadreas composed it as the music for choreographer Kate Wallich's contemporary-dance work 'The Sun Still Burns Here' — which is why it stands as his most abstract, largely instrumental record, closer to a suite than to pop.

media.label: Perfume Genius — The Sun Still Burns Here

cards.hook: He started with a hushed 2010 debut — and grew into towering art-pop
cards.story: Mike Hadreas released his first album as Perfume Genius, Learning, in 2010 — spare, quiet, piano-led songs on Matador. Over the following albums, from Too Bright to No Shape to Glory (2025), the project swelled into lush, physical art-pop without ever losing the tenderness that started it.

cards.hook: An album that refused to leave the US charts for more than a decade straight
cards.story: Pink Floyd found their definitive shape with The Dark Side of the Moon in 1973, a single continuous meditation on time, money and madness. It became one of the best-selling albums ever made and lodged itself on the American charts for a run measured in years, not weeks. Few records have ever proven so unwilling to disappear.

media.label: Search: Dark Side of the Moon Time

sources.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
Pink Floyd's 'Dark Side of the Moon': Most Weeks on Billboard 200 By Title

cards.hook: The band was recording a song about Syd Barrett when Barrett himself walked in unrecognized
cards.story: Wish You Were Here (1975) was built around Shine On You Crazy Diamond, a tribute to founding songwriter Syd Barrett, who had left years earlier as his mental health declined. During the sessions Barrett unexpectedly turned up at the studio, so changed in appearance that his old bandmates struggled to recognize him at first. The album became one of rock's most quietly devastating farewells to a friend.

sources.title: Wish You Were Here (Pink Floyd album)
When Syd Barrett Visited a Pink Floyd Recording Session

cards.hook: The classic four reunited on one stage in 2005 — and never played together again
cards.story: At Live 8 on 2 July 2005, Roger Waters rejoined David Gilmour, Nick Mason and Richard Wright for a short set in London's Hyde Park. It was the first time Waters had performed with Pink Floyd since he departed in the mid-1980s. Barrett died the following year and Wright in 2008, making it the classic lineup's final performance together.

cards.hook: Pink Floyd's only chart-topping single was banned by a government that heard rebellion in it
cards.story: Another Brick in the Wall, Part 2, from The Wall (1979), became the band's only single to reach number one in both the UK and the US. Its chant against rigid schooling struck a nerve well beyond Britain: in 1980 South Africa's apartheid government banned it after black students adopted it during protests. A children's choir sings the song's famous refrain.

media.label: Another Brick in the Wall, Part Two (official)

sources.title: The Number Ones: Pink Floyd's "Another Brick In The Wall (Part II)"

cards.hook: Portishead have made three studio albums — every single one peaked at No. 2 in the UK
cards.story: Dummy (1994) climbed to No. 2 on the UK Albums Chart and stayed on the chart for 111 weeks. Portishead (1997) reached No. 2. Then came the long silence — eleven years between studio albums — before Third (2008) arrived and landed at No. 2 as well. Three records across fourteen years, each one stopping exactly one rung from the top.

cards.hook: Dummy beat Definitely Maybe to the 1995 Mercury Music Prize
cards.story: Portishead's debut won the 1995 Mercury Music Prize over a shortlist that included Oasis' Definitely Maybe, PJ Harvey's To Bring You My Love and Leftfield's Leftism. Released in August 1994 on Go!
