Beat — three years after Geoff Barrow and Beth Gibbons met on an Enterprise Allowance course in Bristol — Dummy turned crackle, spy-film strings and Gibbons' wounded voice into a defining record of the Bristol sound they shaped alongside Massive Attack and Tricky.

cards.hook: Portishead's first show in seven years was a war-relief benefit in their hometown
cards.story: On 2 May 2022, Portishead stepped onto the O2 Academy Bristol stage for HELP! — a War Child UK benefit for children affected by the war in Ukraine — their first performance since 2015, on a bill with fellow Bristol band IDLES. The five-song set reached back across their whole catalog: Mysterons, Magic Doors, Wandering Star, The Rip, Roads. Three years later they gathered for a cause again, recording 'Roads' at Bristol's The Cube for Together For Palestine in September 2025.

media.label: Portishead at the 2022 War Child benefit

sources.title: Portishead Concert Setlist at Help - A War Child Benefit Show 2022
Watch Portishead perform live for the first time since 2015
Watch Portishead Play Their First Show In 7 Years At War Child UK Benefit In Bristol
Portishead Concert Setlist at The Cube, Bristol on September 2, 2025

cards.hook: Portishead covered ABBA for a dystopian film — then gave the streaming profits to charity
cards.story: Portishead recorded a stark, drumless reading of ABBA's 'SOS' for Ben Wheatley's film High-Rise. Years later, in 2021, the band put the cover on SoundCloud — with all streaming profits donated to charity. It remains one of the very few recordings they have released since Third (2008): the others include 2009's Amnesty single 'Chase the Tear' and 2025's 'Roads' for Together For Palestine.

sources.title: Portishead Releases ABBA 'SOS' Cover Exclusively on SoundCloud
Portishead release ABBA 'SOS' cover in aid of mental health charity Mind

cards.hook: A six-minute mock-opera with no chorus that held the UK top spot for nine straight weeks
cards.story: Recorded for A Night at the Opera in 1975, 'Bohemian Rhapsody' stitched a ballad, an operatic passage and a hard-rock finale into one track. It topped the UK Singles Chart for nine weeks, and its promotional film is routinely cited among the works that helped popularise the music video as a form.

sources.title: A Night at the Opera (Queen album)

cards.hook: Twenty minutes at Live Aid in 1985 that keeps topping lists of rock's greatest live sets
cards.story: Queen took the stage at Live Aid in 1985, blazing through a compressed medley of their biggest songs. The set is frequently ranked among the greatest live performances in rock history, and it remains the case cited when people call Queen the era's best live band.

cards.hook: The bassist wrote it, a Chic groove sparked it, and it became Queen's biggest US hit
cards.story: 'Another One Bites the Dust', from 1980's The Game, was written by bassist John Deacon, whose funk-driven bassline was inspired by Chic's 'Good Times'. The track pushed Queen into disco-adjacent territory and became one of their most successful singles in the United States.

media.label: Another One Bites the Dust (official video)

cards.hook: A video where the band did the housework in drag
cards.story: The 1984 clip for 'I Want To Break Free' opened with Queen dressed in women's clothing. The camp visual became a favourite with fans around the world.

media.label: I Want To Break Free (official video)

cards.hook: Before the world knew them as Radiohead, they were a band called On a Friday
cards.story: Five bandmates from Abingdon in Oxfordshire — Thom Yorke, Jonny Greenwood, Colin Greenwood, Ed O'Brien, and Philip Selway — started out under the name On a Friday before renaming themselves Radiohead. What followed was a career spent building, and then reinventing, a sound that would reshape rock.

cards.hook: The 1997 album that gave us Paranoid Android and Karma Police
cards.story: Released in 1997, OK Computer followed The Bends with something colder and stranger — a portrait of technology and unease at the edge of a new century. Its singles, from Paranoid Android to Karma Police, still define the era.

cards.hook: Radiohead let you name your own price — and rewrote how music gets sold
cards.story: In 2007 Radiohead released In Rainbows as a pay-what-you-want download, letting fans choose what to pay before it saw a physical release. The move stunned the industry and became one of the most discussed release strategies in music history. It remains a reference point whenever artists debate how to distribute their work.

cards.hook: After seven years off the stage, Radiohead returned to play in the round
cards.story: In November 2025 Radiohead came back to live performance for the first time in seven years, with a run of arena shows staged in the round across several European cities. Drawing from across their catalog, they treated no two nights the same. It was the band's first tour since 2018.

sources.title: Radiohead Concert Setlist at Movistar Arena, Madrid on November 4, 2025

cards.hook: Rage Against the Machine beat The X Factor to Christmas No. 1
cards.story: By 2009, The X Factor had owned the UK's Christmas No. 1 for four straight years. A fan-organised Facebook campaign pushed Rage Against the Machine's 1992 single 'Killing in the Name' to re-enter the Official Singles Chart at No. 1 the week of December 20, 2009, beating that year's X Factor winner Joe McElderry to the festive top spot — one of the first viral grassroots chart campaigns of the social-media era.

sources.title: Christmas Number 1 Flashback 2009: Rage Against The Machine take on The X Factor...and win

cards.hook: Rage Against the Machine's two Grammys came from deep cuts, not singles
cards.story: Rage Against the Machine won two Grammy Awards for Best Metal or Hard Rock Performance: 'Tire Me' from Evil Empire at the 1997 ceremony, and 'Guerrilla Radio' from The Battle of Los Angeles at the 2001 ceremony — recognition built on the band's heaviest album tracks rather than its best-known singles.

sources.title: Rage Against the Machine — Grammy Awards artist page

cards.hook: Rage Against the Machine's Rock Hall induction had only one member on stage
cards.story: Rage Against the Machine was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame as Performers in 2023, inducted by Ice-T — but Tom Morello was the only member of the band to accept in person, reflecting the band's fractured state between its 2022 reunion tour and its final breakup announcement two months later.

sources.title: Rage Against the Machine | Rock & Roll Hall of Fame

cards.hook: Two guys built a band with no singer and no gaps
cards.story: Mike Stroud and Evan Mast formed Ratatat in Brooklyn, trading vocals for layered electric guitars harmonized into fanfares over chewy synth bass and drum-machine strut. Their self-titled debut arrived in 2004, laying down a blueprint they'd spend the next decade sharpening.

cards.hook: Their instrumental fingerprints are on a Diamond-certified Kid Cudi hit
cards.story: Ratatat's reach spread far past their own records. They helped build Kid Cudi's 'Pursuit of Happiness' alongside MGMT, and the track went on to become one of the most certified songs Cudi has released — earning a Diamond plaque in the United States. It's a fitting side-story for a band whose instrumentals became a quiet backbone of hip-hop.

media.label: Search: Kid Cudi — Pursuit of Happiness

cards.hook: The album that sharpened their formula turns 20 in 2026
cards.story: Classics, released in 2006, tightened everything Ratatat did well. Two decades on, it still reads as the moment the duo's guitars-as-synthesizers idea clicked fully into place.

cards.hook: After five quiet years they came back with a chrome-plated fanfare
cards.story: Following LP4 in 2010, Ratatat went silent for half a decade before returning with Magnifique in 2015. The 2015 single 'Cream on Chrome' brought back the harmonized-guitar strut.

cards.hook: Blood Sugar Sex Magik was recorded in a mansion where Houdini allegedly lived
cards.story: For their 1991 breakthrough, producer Rick Rubin moved Red Hot Chili Peppers out of the studio entirely — into The Mansion, a Laurel Canyon house where the magician Harry Houdini allegedly once lived. The album that came out of it, Blood Sugar Sex Magik, carried 'Under the Bridge' and 'Give It Away', which became the band's first No. 1 single on a Billboard chart and won the Grammy for Best Hard Rock Performance.

sources.title: Red Hot Chili Peppers — Grammy artist page

cards.hook: Their first Billboard No. 1 debut took 23 years — with a 28-song double album
cards.story: Stadium Arcadium (2006) sold 442,000 US copies in its first week and debuted at No. 1 on the Billboard 200 — the first chart-topping debut of the band's career, more than two decades in. It also won the Grammy for Best Rock Album. Six years later, the band was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.

sources.title: Red Hot Chili Peppers — Grammy artist page
Red Hot Chili Peppers — Rock & Roll Hall of Fame

cards.hook: Santigold released her debut album under a different name
cards.story: Santi White's 2008 debut album was released as Santogold, and she performed under that name for its first year. In 2009 she changed her stage name to Santigold — the name every release since has carried, and the one MusicBrainz lists as canonical, with Santogold recorded as a former alias rather than a separate artist.

cards.hook: Before Santigold existed, she wrote an entire album for someone else
cards.story: Before her own solo career, Santi White worked inside the industry she'd go on to reshape: she did A&R work at Epic Records and sang in the punk band Stiffed. In 2001, she wrote soul singer Res's debut album How I Do in its entirety alongside producer Martin 'Doc' McKinney — a record that moved through reggae, trip-hop and rock, previewing the genre-hopping that would define her own music years later.

sources.title: Tribute to Res' 2001 Debut Album, 'How I Do'

cards.hook: They took their name from a baby — the frontman's newborn sister
cards.story: Sigur Rós came together in Reykjavík in 1994, and named themselves after Sigurrós, the newborn sister of singer Jónsi Birgisson. Around Jónsi's bowed guitar and falsetto they built a slow, weightless sound rooted in the Icelandic landscape. Their 1999 album Ágætis byrjun broke them worldwide and became the first record to win the Shortlist Music Prize.

cards.hook: The song the BBC used to sell Planet Earth to the world
cards.story: Hoppípolla, from 2005's Takk..., became Sigur Rós's most beloved song after the BBC used it to promote the landmark nature series Planet Earth. The exposure pushed the band to re-release it as a single in 2006, and it has since soundtracked everything from documentaries to weddings.

sources.title: Planet Earth II: Sigur Rós share new version of 'Hoppípolla' for TV series

cards.hook: An album whose title is nothing but a pair of empty brackets
cards.story: In 2002 Sigur Rós released ( ), an album whose title is nothing but a pair of empty brackets — one of the boldest gestures in their catalog.

cards.hook: After a decade of near-silence, they returned with their eighth album
cards.story: In June 2023 Sigur Rós broke a ten-year album gap with ÁTTA, their eighth studio record and their first since 2013's Kveikur.

cards.hook: At thirteen he became the youngest solo artist ever to top the Billboard Hot 100
cards.story: Stevland Hardaway Morris was born in Saginaw, Michigan, and signed to Motown's Tamla label as a child prodigy billed as Little Stevie Wonder. In 1963 his live recording of Fingertips reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100, making him the youngest solo artist to top the chart. The child stardom was only the beginning of a career that would reshape soul music.

cards.hook: Superstition grew out of a studio jam intended for a rock guitarist
cards.story: One of Stevie Wonder's most enduring grooves, Superstition, appeared on Talking Book in 1972. The riff took shape during sessions where Jeff Beck was involved, and the song ultimately became a Wonder release that reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100. Its clavinet hook remains one of the most recognizable sounds in soul.

sources.title: How Stevie Wonder's Apology Led to One of Jeff Beck's Signature Tunes

cards.hook: The only artist to win Album of the Year with three album releases in a row
cards.story: During his celebrated classic period, Stevie Wonder took home the Grammy for Album of the Year three times for consecutive releases — Innervisions, Fulfillingness' First Finale, and Songs in the Key of Life. No other artist has matched that feat of winning with three consecutive album releases. It capped a run of albums on which he wrote, produced, and played most of the instruments himself.

sources.title: Grammy Award for Album of the Year
Songs In The Key Of Stevie Wonder

cards.hook: He wrote a birthday song to help win a national holiday for Martin Luther King Jr.
cards.story: In 1980 Stevie Wonder became a leading voice in the campaign to make Martin Luther King Jr.'s birthday a national holiday in the United States. He released Happy Birthday that year on Hotter than July as a rallying song for the effort. Martin Luther King Jr. Day eventually became a federal holiday.

cards.hook: Sublime's biggest album came out two months after its singer died
cards.story: Bradley Nowell, Sublime's singer and guitarist, died of a heroin overdose on May 25, 1996. The band's self-titled third album arrived two months later and went on to sell millions of copies, carried by 'What I Got', 'Santeria' and 'Wrong Way' — songs whose creator never heard the reception they received.

cards.hook: Bradley Nowell's son now fronts Sublime — the band debuted its new lineup at Coachella
cards.story: Nearly three decades after the band went silent, surviving members Eric Wilson and Bud Gaugh reunited under the Sublime name in 2024 with a new singer and guitarist: Jakob Nowell, Bradley Nowell's son. The lineup debuted publicly at Coachella that year.

cards.hook: Thirty years after their last album, Sublime released a new one
cards.story: In 2026, the reunited Sublime — Wilson, Gaugh and Jakob Nowell — released Until the Sun Explodes, a full-length album and the band's first new full-length since the 1996 self-titled record that followed Bradley Nowell's death.

sources.title: Sublime Readies Fourth Era With Until the Sun Explodes

cards.hook: Sufjan promised an album for all 50 states — and later admitted it was a gimmick
cards.story: After Michigan (2003), Sufjan Stevens announced the Fifty States Project: one album per US state. He completed exactly two — Michigan and Illinois — and later admitted the plan had been a 'promotional gimmick' he never seriously intended to finish. The gimmick still produced Illinois (2005), Pitchfork's best album of 2005 and a summit of modern American songwriting.

cards.hook: Sufjan performed at the Oscars — nominated for the Call Me by Your Name song
cards.story: 'Mystery of Love', written for Call Me by Your Name, was nominated for Best Original Song at the 90th Academy Awards, and Stevens performed it on the Oscars stage on March 4, 2018. That televised performance is also, fittingly, among his last documented live appearances — he has not toured since 2018.

cards.hook: Illinois became a Broadway musical — and won a Tony
cards.story: In 2024, Sufjan Stevens' album Illinois was transformed into Illinoise, a Broadway musical at the St. James Theatre directed and choreographed by Justin Peck. It was nominated for four Tony Awards including Best Musical, and won the Tony for Peck's choreography — a 2005 indie concept album, reborn as award-winning theater.

cards.hook: SUUNS made a whole album with another Montreal band — recorded in a single week
cards.story: In 2015, SUUNS stepped outside their own catalog to release Suuns and Jerusalem In My Heart, a full collaborative album with fellow Montreal experimental act Jerusalem In My Heart. The tracks were recorded together over a single week — a genuine group effort, not a remix trade.

media.label: SUUNS and Jerusalem In My Heart

cards.hook: SUUNS' second album got a Polaris Music Prize longlist nod
cards.story: Images du futur (2013), the band's colder, more electronic second album, was longlisted for the Polaris Music Prize — Canada's award for the year's best album regardless of genre or sales. Seven years later they signed to Joyful Noise Recordings, a relationship that has produced the Fiction EP, The Witness and The Breaks.

cards.hook: Tame Impala is a band onstage — but on record it's basically one person in isolated Perth
cards.story: Tame Impala looks like a full psychedelic rock band under one of the most celebrated light shows in music, but the recordings are essentially the work of a single man: Kevin Parker, who writes, performs, and produces the music himself. He builds it in Perth, Western Australia, one of the most remote major cities on Earth — an isolation many hear in the music's dreamy self-containment. The home-recorded jams became 2010's debut 'InnerSpeaker', and the project only grew from there.

media.label: Feels Like We Only Go Backwards (official video)

cards.hook: The album where Kevin Parker ditched fuzz guitars for synths — and became a festival headliner
cards.story: Tame Impala's first two albums leaned on swirling psychedelic guitar, but 2015's 'Currents' swapped much of that for synthesizers and dancefloor basslines. The pivot reshaped the project's sound and its scale, turning Parker into an arena and festival draw and an in-demand collaborator. Songs like 'Let It Happen' became defining tracks of the era.

media.label: The Less I Know the Better (official video)

cards.hook: Rihanna heard a Tame Impala song and put her own version on her album Anti
cards.story: Tame Impala's 'New Person, Same Old Mistakes' closes 'Currents' — and it caught Rihanna's ear. She recorded her own version, retitled 'Same Ol' Mistakes', for her 2016 album 'Anti'. It's one of the clearest signs of how Parker's pivot to synth-pop made him a magnet for the pop world's biggest names.

media.label: Feels Like We Only Go Backwards (official video)

cards.hook: Tame Impala's fifth album takes its cues from Western Australia's outdoor rave scene
cards.story: In October 2025 Kevin Parker released 'Deadbeat', Tame Impala's fifth album, drawing on the outdoor rave culture — the 'bush doofs' — of Western Australia. Its harder, dancefloor-leaning material powered a world arena tour running through 2026, from European arenas in spring to North American dates in the summer.

cards.hook: Four Liverpudlians, a Hamburg boot camp, and a drummer swap that made history
cards.story: The Beatles came together in Liverpool in 1960, sharpening their sound through grueling residencies in Hamburg's clubs and at the Cavern. The classic lineup locked in when Ringo Starr joined in 1962, replacing Pete Best. Their debut single 'Love Me Do' charted in Britain late that year, and within eighteen months Beatlemania had taken hold.

cards.hook: 55,600 fans, one stadium, and the concert that invented the stadium show
cards.story: On August 15, 1965, The Beatles played New York's Shea Stadium in front of roughly 55,600 people, a pop-concert attendance record at the time. The scale was unprecedented — no band had attempted a show this big. The screaming crowds largely drowned out the music, a sign of the touring pressures that would soon push the band off the road.

cards.hook: The Beatles' last-ever gig lasted 42 minutes — and the police shut it down
cards.story: On January 30, 1969, The Beatles climbed to the roof of their Apple Corps headquarters at 3 Savile Row in London for an unannounced set. It ran about 42 minutes before police, responding to noise complaints, brought it to a halt. It was the band's final public performance, captured in the Let It Be film and revisited in Peter Jackson's Get Back.

cards.hook: A 1970s cassette demo became the last Beatles single — and it hit UK No. 1 in 2023
cards.story: 'Now and Then', built from a late-1970s John Lennon home demo, was released on November 2, 2023, as the last Beatles song. Machine-learning audio restoration cleanly isolated Lennon's vocal from the old cassette, letting Paul McCartney and Ringo Starr finish the track. It went to number one on the UK Singles Chart.

sources.title: The Beatles' Now And Then is UK's Official Number 1 song in record-breaking return

cards.hook: The Books built four albums out of other people's discarded tapes
cards.story: The Books formed in 1999 after cellist Paul de Jong invited his neighbor Nick Zammuto to dinner and played him pieces of his enormous collection of found audio. The duo built their entire catalog — from Thought for Food (2002) to The Way Out (2010) — around samples of obscure speech and salvaged recordings, woven through live guitar and cello into something no genre label ever quite fit.

sources.title: The Books: Making Music Through Found Sound
The Books: The Lemon of Pink — review

cards.hook: Their concerts ran in sync with films they cut themselves
cards.story: The Books were as much a video band as an audio one: they performed live against their own video collages, cut from the same thrift-store archives as the songs, timed so sound and image landed together. The band disbanded in 2012 — Zammuto continues under his own name, de Jong solo — leaving a sealed, four-album world.

media.label: The Books live with video projections

sources.title: Nick Zammuto talks about ending The Books

cards.hook: Classically trained on cello and violin, three New Orleans sisters bent tempo and key on national radio
cards.story: Martha, Connee, and Helvetia 'Vet' Boswell grew up in uptown New Orleans with classical training, then soaked up the city's jazz and blues. Working from Connee's arrangements, they reshaped hit songs — shifting tempo mid-song, sliding between major and minor, and blending three voices so tightly they sounded like one. When they reached national radio and began recording for Brunswick in 1931, little else on the air sounded like them.

cards.hook: They shared the screen with Bing Crosby and Cab Calloway in 1932's The Big Broadcast
cards.story: The trio's national fame put them in Hollywood. In Paramount's The Big Broadcast (1932), a musical revue built around radio stars, the Boswell Sisters appeared alongside Bing Crosby and Cab Calloway. The film captured their unusual sound for audiences who had only heard them over the airwaves.

media.label: Boswell Sisters in The Big Broadcast

cards.hook: The Andrews Sisters openly modeled themselves on the Boswells — who dissolved at the top in 1936
cards.story: The Boswell Sisters' close, jazz-inflected harmony became a blueprint: the Andrews Sisters have long been credited as modeling their style on them. Yet the Boswells' run at the top was short — in 1936 the trio disbanded, and Connee carried on as a major solo star, including celebrated recordings with Bing Crosby.

cards.hook: The Object of My Affection was one of the hits that carried them to their national peak
cards.story: The Boswell Sisters put The Object of My Affection onto the charts in the mid-1930s, one of the string of records that marked their national peak. Their treatment turned the popular tune into a showcase for the sliding tempos and tight harmony that set them apart.

cards.hook: A brand-new band ambushed the world inside a Glastonbury livestream — playing songs nobody had heard
cards.story: The Smile came together in lockdown as Thom Yorke and Jonny Greenwood started trading ideas with jazz drummer Tom Skinner. They revealed themselves in May 2021 inside Glastonbury's 'Live at Worthy Farm' streamed event, performing a set of entirely unheard material. It was less a soft launch than a surprise attack — fans tuned in expecting a festival broadcast and got a full new trio.

media.label: The Smile at Glastonbury's Live at Worthy Farm

cards.hook: Their debut single was a snarling, odd-metered punk gut-punch — not the ballad you might expect from Radiohead's core
cards.story: The Smile's first proper releases arrived in 2021, with 'You Will Never Work in Television Again' landing as a fast, jagged burst of post-punk energy, followed by the more spacious 'The Smoke.' Both fed into the debut album 'A Light for Attracting Attention,' released on XL Recordings in 2022 and produced by longtime Radiohead collaborator Nigel Godrich. The record leaned on krautrock, post-punk and Greenwood's orchestral writing.

media.label: You Will Never Work in Television Again (official video)

sources.title: The Smile: A Light for Attracting Attention

cards.hook: Two studio albums in a single year — a pace their day job never approached
cards.story: After the debut and a 2022 live album from the Montreux Jazz Festival, The Smile moved fast. 'Wall of Eyes' arrived in January 2024, produced by Sam Petts-Davies and greeted with some of the strongest reviews of Yorke and Greenwood's late career. That October came 'Cutouts,' drawn largely from the same fertile sessions — making two full studio records inside one calendar year.

cards.hook: The drummer who reshaped this band came from the front line of UK jazz, not from Radiohead's world
cards.story: The Smile's looser, more elastic feel owes much to Tom Skinner, a London drummer who arrived from the UK jazz scene rather than the rock circuit. He was widely known for his work with Sons of Kemet, and his playing gives the trio its krautrock nerve and jazz swing.

cards.hook: Thievery Corporation started because world music wasn't danceable
cards.story: Rob Garza and Eric Hilton met in 1995 at Hilton's Washington, D.C. club, the Eighteenth Street Lounge. In Garza's words: 'We wanted to find a way to make it a bit more modern and incorporate electronic production and bring it into the future' — bossa nova, reggae, Indian sitar and old European soundtrack music, reworked with a danceable electronic beat. Their first day working together, they finished a song that ended up on their debut album, and eventually moved into the top floor of the Lounge itself to keep recording.

cards.hook: Thievery Corporation stayed in Washington, DC on purpose
cards.story: Unlike many producers who relocate to New York or Los Angeles once they've built a career, Garza and Hilton have stayed based in Washington, D.C. since forming the group in 1995 — running their own label and studio from the same city where they started, rather than the industry's usual coastal hubs.

cards.hook: Twenty years ago this month, Radiohead's singer slipped out the side door with a laptop album
cards.story: Released in July 2006, The Eraser was Thom Yorke's first solo record — clipped beats and processed piano assembled largely on laptops with longtime Radiohead producer Nigel Godrich. Rather than tour it as a one-off, Yorke later assembled the band Atoms for Peace to bring the songs to a live stage. Two decades on, it still reads as the blueprint for everything glitchy and anxious he'd chase alone.

cards.hook: He released an album through BitTorrent with a paywall — skipping the industry entirely
cards.story: In 2014, Yorke put out Tomorrow's Modern Boxes not through stores or the usual streaming rails but as a pay-gated BitTorrent bundle, an experiment in bypassing the industry's channels. It was another swerve from an artist who keeps refusing to repeat himself. The opening track, Last I Heard (…He Was Circling the Drain), captures its hushed, nocturnal electronics.

media.label: Last I Heard (…He Was Circling the Drain) — official video

cards.hook: Radiohead's frontman scored a horror remake — and kept working in film
cards.story: For Luca Guadagnino's 2018 Suspiria remake, Yorke composed the score, trading loops for choir, piano and dread. He kept working in cinema afterward, writing music for the Italian thriller Confidenza. His screen work runs parallel to his solo albums, another outlet for the same restless ear.

cards.hook: In 2025 Yorke made a whole album with an electronic producer he'd orbited for years
cards.story: Tall Tales, released in 2025, is a full collaborative album between Thom Yorke and electronic producer Mark Pritchard — an artist Yorke had worked with before. Its singles Dialing In and Four Ways in Time show the pairing's mix of woozy electronics and Yorke's unmistakable falsetto. It's one more branch on his ever-expanding collaboration web, alongside The Smile.

media.label: Four Ways in Time — official video

cards.hook: Tipper is so obsessed with sound that he made an album built for surround speakers
cards.story: David Tipper's whole reputation rests on detail. He is among the electronic producers most identified with surround and multichannel audio: his album Surrounded was conceived entirely as a surround-sound listening experience, a record you were meant to sit inside rather than just hear.

cards.hook: The studio obsessive filled Red Rocks two nights running
cards.story: For all his laptop-scientist reputation, Tipper is a live force in the North American festival scene. In September 2025 he headlined Colorado's Red Rocks Amphitheatre on two consecutive nights — the kind of natural-amphitheatre room usually reserved for the biggest names in music.

sources.title: Tipper at Red Rocks Amphitheatre, Sep 20 2025
Tipper at Red Rocks Amphitheatre, Sep 21 2025

cards.hook: After 13 silent years, Tool returned in 2019 — and put a ten-minute song on the Hot 100
cards.story: Thirteen years separated 10,000 Days from Fear Inoculum, released August 30, 2019. The album debuted at number one on the Billboard 200, its sprawling ten-minute title track became the longest song ever to reach the Billboard Hot 100 at the time — a record it held until 2023 — and the seething 7empest later won the 2020 Grammy for Best Metal Performance. It was a defiant reminder that a band can vanish for over a decade and come back on top.

sources.title: Tool's 'Fear Inoculum' Is The Longest Hot 100 Hit

cards.hook: The Lateralus title track was built around the Fibonacci sequence — and the album still hit number one
cards.story: Lateralus arrived in 2001 and debuted atop the Billboard 200. Its spiraling nine-minute title track famously arranges its syllables and rhythms around the Fibonacci sequence, turning math into a slow-building meditation. The album also delivered Schism, whose knotty time signatures won Tool the Grammy for Best Metal Performance and cemented their status as progressive-metal architects.

sources.title: Why some Tool fans think their Lateralus album is hiding a huge secret

cards.hook: Before the lineup that lasted forever, Tool were LA post-hardcore outsiders with stop-motion nightmares
cards.story: Tool formed in Los Angeles in 1990, and their 1993 debut album Undertow paired coiled heaviness with Adam Jones's unsettling stop-motion videos for Sober and Prison Sex. In 1995 Justin Chancellor replaced original bassist Paul D'Amour, completing the lineup that has held ever since. The following year's Ænima brought a first Grammy, with the title track winning Best Metal Performance.

cards.hook: Tortoise are credited as one of the prime forces behind post-rock — a genre that barely had a name when they started
cards.story: Tortoise formed in Chicago in 1990 around a rotating cast of multi-instrumentalists — nearly every member plays nearly every instrument. Critics reached for a new word to describe what they were doing: Tortoise have been consistently credited, as part of the Chicago school, as one of the prime forces behind the rise of post-rock in the 1990s.

cards.hook: TNT won over critics for sounding like nothing else in 1998
cards.story: Tortoise's third album, TNT (1998), folded funk, ambient and the band's ongoing krautrock influence into their established postmodern range, and was critically acclaimed on release for that very range — a record that has only grown in stature since.

cards.hook: One Las Vegas multi-instrumentalist hides behind two names — and a whole band
cards.story: Trust in the Sun is a Las Vegas–based band and recording collective that folds jazz, soul, funk and pop into what the group calls a buttery fusion. At its center is composer and multi-instrumentalist Stefano Kajatt, who also releases music under the name Aplete. The project's catalogue of singles lives on Bandcamp, passing through a rotating cast of players.

media.label: Trust in the Sun on Bandcamp

cards.hook: A single called Orange Juice got the full official-video treatment
cards.story: Among Trust in the Sun's Bandcamp singles is Orange Juice, one of the tracks the collective chose to give a proper official music video. It's a good entry point to the group's genre-blurring, buttery mix of jazz, soul, funk and pop.

cards.hook: The singles list doubles as a guest book — including a Chris Mendoza feature
cards.story: Trust in the Sun works as a collective, and that shows up in its credits. The single When It Was features Chris Mendoza, sitting alongside tracks like Show Me How, Sex Magic, Empress, Find Love and City of Stars in the group's Bandcamp catalogue.

media.label: Search: When It Was (feat. Chris Mendoza)

cards.hook: tUnE-yArDs' breakthrough album was named the best of 2011 by a major critics' poll
cards.story: w h o k i l l (2011) — built the same way as everything Merrill Garbus makes, from loop pedals, ukulele and layered vocals — was named the number one album of 2011 on The Village Voice's Pazz & Jop critics' poll. Pitchfork later named it among the 100 Best Albums of the Decade So Far.

cards.hook: tUnE-yArDs is built from loop pedals and a ukulele — and one songwriter's voice, multiplied
cards.story: Merrill Garbus's project is defined by its method: loop pedals, ukulele, lo-fi percussion and layered vocals, built live into songs that sound like a full band from just her and bassist Nate Brenner. The stylized capitalization of the name — tUnE-yArDs — carries through every release, right down to the band's own branding.

cards.hook: Wye Oak's drummer plays the bass line with one hand — while drumming with the other three limbs
cards.story: Live, the Baltimore duo is a magic trick. Andy Stack plays drums with his feet and right hand while his left hand plays bass and keyboard parts, and Jenn Wasner covers voice and guitar — two people making a full band's sound in plain sight. The band is named after Maryland's former state tree, a giant white oak that fell in 2002.

cards.hook: 'Civilian' escaped the album — all the way to The Walking Dead
cards.story: The title track of Wye Oak's 2011 breakthrough Civilian traveled far beyond the indie circuit: it appeared in the season 2 trailer and in the closing scene of a season 2 episode of The Walking Dead, and in the film Safety Not Guaranteed. After that peak, the band's next move was to delete their signature sound entirely — Shriek (2014) was written without guitar.

cards.hook: Half of Wye Oak spent 2019 on stage with Bon Iver
cards.story: Jenn Wasner has never been just one band. Alongside Wye Oak she records as Flock of Dimes — with an NPR Tiny Desk concert to its name — and in 2019 she joined Bon Iver's touring band as a guitarist. All of it feeds back into the duo she and Andy Stack keep reinventing.

sources.title: Flock of Dimes: Tiny Desk (Home) Concert
